title: Household Survey Shows Sustained Rise in Reported and Expected Spending Growth

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: The Federal Reserve Bank of New York's December 2021 Survey of Consumer Expectations found median monthly household spending growth rose to 5.1% year-over-year, the highest since the series began in December 2014, while median year-ahead spending growth expectations reached 4.6%, the highest since August 2015. The share of households reporting large purchases declined from August but remained above December 2020 levels, with households expressing lower likelihood of making large purchases going forward despite higher spending expectations.
key_points: Median monthly household spending increased 5.1% YoY in December 2021, up from 4.7% in August 2021 and 1.6% in December 2020
Median year-ahead expected overall spending growth rose to 4.6% in December from 4.2% in August, reaching series high since August 2015
Median expected growth in essential spending reached 5.4% in December, series high since August 2015, up from 4.1% in December 2020
58.1% of households reported making at least one large purchase in the past four months, down from 62.6% in August but above 54.6% in December 2020
Expected spending responses to 10% income increase show 31.4% would pay down debt (down from 36.3% pre-pandemic), 48.8% saved or invested (up from 44.5%)
Shares reporting large purchases on home repairs, furniture, homes, and vehicles all declined from August 2021 levels
why_it_matters: This survey provides insight into consumer spending behavior and inflation expectations at the household level. Rising spending growth expectations combined with lower reported likelihood of large purchases suggests consumers anticipate higher prices, which could reinforce inflationary pressures. The data shows consumers are allocating more of any income increase to saving rather than debt repayment compared to pre-pandemic.
macro_context: This release provides data on consumer spending expectations from the December 2021 Survey of Consumer Expectations. Rising spending growth expectations (4.6% year-ahead) combined with lower reported likelihood of large purchases suggests households anticipate elevated prices, which could reinforce inflationary pressures. Historical record from early post-pandemic period (January 2022) — serves as a benchmark for consumer behavior during that era.
